sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 49, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Starting level 1. The board shows a blue background with several colored objects. Need to understand the layout and goal.
- Plan: Inspect the segmentation to identify all objects, their colors, positions, and relationships.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE didn't change anything. The dark gray shape still has 18 pixels and the yellow strip has 18 pixels. Let me reconsider the mechanics.
Looking at the initial state:
- Character at [[15, 18], [15, 26], [23, 26]] - this is an L-shape (horizontal bar at row 15, cols 18-26, vertical bar at cols 26, rows 15-23)
- Dark gray at [[15, 36], [15, 44], [17, 44]] - this is also an L-shape (horizontal bar at row 15, cols 36-44, vertical bar at cols 44, rows 15-17)
Wait, the dark gray initial boundary is [[15, 36], [15, 44], [17, 44]] which is only 3 points. That's a small L-shape. But it has 45 pixels.
Actually, looking more carefully at the initial state, the character is at [[15, 18], [15, 26], [23, 26]] with 40 pixels. This is an L-shape: horizontal bar from (15,18) to (15,26) and vertical bar from (15,26) to (23,26).
The dark gray shape at [[15, 36], [15, 44], [17, 44]] with 45 pixels is also an L-shape: horizontal bar from (15,36) to (15,44) and vertical bar from (15,44) to (17,44)... but that's only 3 rows tall, which doesn't match 45 pixels.
Let me reconsider. The boundary points are clockwise corner points, not all cells. So the dark gray L-shape with boundary [[15, 36], [15, 44], [17, 44]] would be:
- Top edge: row 15, cols 36-44 (9 cells)
- Right edge: col 44, rows 15-17 (3 cells)
- Bottom edge: row 17, cols 36-44 (9 cells)
- Left edge: col 36, rows 15-17 (3 cells)
That's a 3x9 rectangle = 27 cells, not 45. Something's off.
Actually wait, the boundary is clockwise outer-perimeter corner points. So [[15, 36], [15, 44], [17, 44]] means:
- Start at (15, 36) - top-left
- Go to (15, 44) - top-right
- Go to (17, 44) - bottom-right
This describes a shape that's 3 rows tall (15-17) and 9 cols wide (36-44), but it's an L-shape, not a rectangle. The L-shape would have:
- Horizontal bar: row 15, cols 36-44 (9 cells)
- Vertical bar: col 44, rows 15-? 
With 45 pixels and 9 cells in the horizontal bar, the vertical bar would have 36 cells, meaning it extends from row 15 to row 50 (36 cells). So the boundary [[15, 36], [15, 44], [17, 44]] is incomplete - it should have more points for the full L-shape.
Actually, the boundary only shows the first 3 points in my print. Let me look at the full boundary.
Anyway, the key observation is:
1. The character "eats" parts of the dark gray shape when overlapping
2. The yellow strip on the right edge shrinks as the dark gray shape shrinks
3. The yellow strip went from 64 to 18 pixels, and the dark gray went from 45 to 18 pixels
4. The yellow L-shape at bottom right (45 pixels) remains unchanged
I think the goal is to completely "eat" the dark gray L-shape. The character needs to move over all parts of it. Currently the dark gray has 18 pixels remaining, all in a vertical bar at [[51, 27], [51, 29], [56, 29], [56, 27]].
The character is at [[48, 27], [48, 35], [56, 35], [56, 33], [51, 33], [50, 32], [50, 27]]. The character overlaps with the dark gray shape. But the dark gray hasn't been eaten yet.
Wait, maybe the eating happens when the character moves, not when it's stationary. Let me try moving the character while it's overlapping the dark gray shape.
Actually, looking at the history, the dark gray shape went from 45 to 18 pixels when I moved the character from the right side to the left side (crossing the divider). So the eating happens when the character crosses over the dark gray shape's cells.
The remaining dark gray is at [[51, 27], [51, 29], [56, 29], [56, 27]] - a vertical bar from row 51 to 56, cols 27-29. The character spans rows 48-56, cols 27-35. So the character already overlaps with this region.
Hmm, but the dark gray hasn't been eaten. Maybe I need to move the character so that it fully covers the dark gray shape's cells. Let me try moving LEFT to push the character more onto the dark gray.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
char = [n for n in seg if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] == 40][0]
dark_gray = [n for n in seg if n['color'] == 'c'][0]

print(f"Char boundary: {char['boundary']}")
print(f"Dark gray boundary: {dark_gray['boundary']}, pixels={dark_gray['pixels']}")

# The dark gray is a vertical bar at cols 27-29, rows 51-56
# The char is at cols 27-35, rows 48-56
# They overlap, but maybe I need to move char LEFT to fully cover the dark gray
# Let me try moving LEFT

action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
